text.title: Mob Programming Patterns
text.summary: Mob variants (TDD, learning, bug-hunt, randori) and session setup for physical and remote environments.

section.title: TDD Mob
section.p: Navigator describes test intent → Driver writes failing test → rotate → new Navigator describes implementation → Driver makes test pass → rotate → refactor together. Benefits: TDD discipline is enforced by the group, tests are clear because the intent is spoken aloud, and design emerges collaboratively.

section.title: Learning Mob
section.p: An expert explains concepts while the team drives hands-on. No pressure for output. Rotation is slower (15-20 min). Questions are encouraged. Used to spread a new technique (TDD, hexagonal architecture, a new framework) across the team in one session.

section.title: Bug-Hunt Mob
section.p: When a critical production bug cannot be solved alone and requires cross-discipline context. Reproduce together → hypothesize causes → test hypotheses systematically → fix collaboratively → add regression test → document root cause. The mob's strength is all relevant context present simultaneously.

section.title: Randori
section.p: One exercise (kata), each person adds one line/change, rotate after each change. Must keep tests green. If stuck, ask for help. Time-boxed at 1-2 hours. Used for learning and spreading patterns through micro-contribution rounds.

list.item: Video: Zoom or Meet with cameras on.
Driving: VS Code Live Share or Tuple for low-latency screen share.
Timer: mobti.me (shared URL visible to all).
Whiteboard: Miro or FigJam for diagrams.
Smaller mob for remote (3-4 people); shorter sessions (60-90 min); more frequent breaks.

rule.statement: Time-box disagreements to 2 minutes, then try one approach and learn from the result. Experiments beat debates because code is cheap to change.
rule.rationale: Endless debate is the most common mob killer after rotation failure. A short time-box forces commitment to action; the retrospective is the right venue for deeper evaluation.

rule.statement: Maintain a parking lot for off-topic items. Review at the retrospective. Never derail the session for an item that can wait 90 minutes.
rule.rationale: Off-topic discussions break flow and signal to the team that the session goal is not respected. The parking lot preserves ideas without cost to focus.

text.reference: Session bootstrapper: WIP branch, log file, draft PR, rotation bell
